description: Thursday 18 June 2026 | Economic Development Science and Innovation Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Business, Science and Innovation Hearing of Evidence: Minister for Economic Growth Hon Nicola Willis Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment Nic Blakeley, Chief Executive and Secretary for Economic Growth Anna Clark, Deputy Secretary, Labour. Science and Enterprise Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Corporate and Digital Shared Services Sharyn Mitchel, Chief Financial Officer Robyn Henderson, General Manager, Economic Growth Andrew Hume, General Manager, Commerce and Consumer Affairs Michael Alp, General Manager, New Zealand Government Procurement Richard Lee, Head of Strategy, Policy, and Governance, New Zealand Government Procurement Iain Cossar, Deputy Secretary, Fuel Response Shar Amner, General Manager, Te Kupenga Māori Economic Policy & Strategic Partnerships Minister for Tourism and Hospitality Hon Louise Upston Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment Nic Blakeley, Chief Executive and Secretary for Economic Growth Anna Clark, Deputy Chief Executive, Labour, Science and Enterprise Saskia Vervoorn, Acting General Manager, Tourism and Hospitality David Talbot, Manager, Investment Management Performance Tom Simcock, Manager, Tourism Stewardship and Systems Deborah Jones, Manager, New Zealand Major Events Harriet Miller, Manager, Tourism Regions and Hospitality Tourism New Zealand René de Monchy, Chief Executive Paul Brock, Board Chair
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Business, Science and Innovation. Minister for Economic Growth. Hon Nicola Willis

chunks.text: Procurement rules to introduce a new New Zealand impact test that means that when government agencies are going out to tender and comparing different proposals, the impact that a tender would have on the New Zealand economy in terms of job creation, training opportunities, value added to this economy, is now required to be weighted as part of that agency's consideration.

Which is to say, don't just look at what's the lowest cost, look at what's the highest value. And when you're considering value, consider New Zealand economic impact. And I've been upfront, I think that'll give a leg up to Kiwi businesses when they're seeking the revenue that can come from the government contracts. When did that come in, sorry? Hold on. Questions to the chair.

Yeah. You have a supplementary? Yeah. Yeah, supplementary on that. And thank you for that, Minister. I was going to ask you a sup on that, which I'll carry on. So on the procurement processes- Mm... the rules have changed in terms of like you just explained. Is it any easier for Kiwi businesses to get those contracts or to actually navigate the processes in terms of just how easy it is to find your way on system or anything like that?

Yeah, look, at the same time- Because we don't-... as we made those changes, we also looked at simplifying the rules. So we reduced the number of the rules, because the way that it had previously operated, you basically needed a PhD to read through them and navigate them. So wherever possible, we've simplified them. In terms of the GET system, the thing that I was really conscious of was that when I had, as minister, asked questions about, well, what proportion of contracts do we think go to Kiwi firms, how many Kiwi firms do we think are

bidding, we had really quite a lack of data on all of that. So the GET system has been updated so that we can capture more data going forward. Not in a position to share that data with members today, but in the future, because of those updates to the GET system, we'll be in a better position to do so. Thank you. A question from Belinda Wilson. I just had a sup on that.

Oh, you have a supplementary? Yeah. And can I just say this before you ask your sup, Vini? I think I might know where you're going because the minister has gone quite wide, so I'll just see what your question is, so. Okay. You can see inside my mind pumping over here. I think I'm able to do that, so let's see. Yeah. You mentioned the New Zealand impact test, Minister.

Mm-hmm. Can you just give me the date when that sort of applied, so when that would start to kick in and that we could start seeing examples of how that would play out here in New Zealand? Yeah. Look, it came into effect on the 1st of December 2025. And so that's the fifth edition of the procurement rules, and that's the ones that were updated with the new economic benefit rule. And so, already you would expect that government agencies, whenever they're going out for tenders, that they are applying those rules, and therefore applying the New

Zealand Economic Impact Test. And I expect in the coming months to get reporting on how that is impacting reporting decisions. Initial reporting shows that 75% of procurements that occurred between December 2025 and February 2026 included New Zealand economic benefits. That's great. Thank you.

All right. Krishna Dangiri Manaak you have a supplement? Yes. Thanks, Madam Chair. Tena koe, Minister. Will you be reinstating Māori progressive procurement targets, given that when they were awarded 3,200 contracts, that was worth 930 million to the Māori and New Zealand economy?

No. Instead, our approach has been to say, in terms of the procurement rules, that looking at how job creation and career progression opportunities will be created in a tender is relevant, and therefore how many New Zealand businesses are being employed in the delivery of a contract is a relevant consideration.

And of course, that supports the Māori workforce and the Māori economy because many of New Zealand's successful and thriving small businesses are Māori businesses. And we can see that there is considerable momentum there with the growth that's been occurring in the Māori economy. And I have very high confidence that given the opportunity to bid for government contracts or be part of government procurement, Māori businesses have every opportunity to succeed.

Okay. A new primary, Ruben Edwards. Yeah. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Minister, for your time this afternoon. Just wanted to look at, you talked earlier in one of your answers about projects being dusted off. Mm-hmm. I wanted to look at industry closures, so manufacturing where there's dust settling on manufacturing. Mm-hmm. We're seeing this across New Zealand.

Heinz Watties has confirmed 300 jobs. McCain shutting a processing plant, hundreds of jobs there. Kaitaia Timber Mills, 200 jobs in the Far North put at risk. So we're seeing this across the country. Can you point to a single appropriation in the budget that'll stop the next factory from closing?

Well, the member will be welcome to a view that says that government budgets should have line items developing individual factories and funding the development of state-owned factories. That's a hark back to a version of communism that I don't support, because I think what you want to see is the private sector choosing to invest in manufacturing facilities and to develop manufacturing facilities.

I, like the member, and I'm sure like all of the members around this table, have been really disappointed to see the closure of those New Zealand food manufacturers with iconic brands, long-standing relationships with local suppliers, long-standing employment of people in communities.

And so when they close or downscale, it is a very significant and sad impact for their communities and for the New Zealand economy, and I fully acknowledge that. What I am given confidence by is the growth, the bounce back that we've seen in manufacturing in the latest GDP data. In particular, what that shows is that for the quarter, manufacturing grew 1.9%, which was above most forecasts. And that was quite specifically in relation to the member's question, not just for non-food manufacturing, but quite specifically for food manufacturing as well.

So that's showing solid growth, and absolutely we want to see that. And again, what that comes back to in terms of government budgets is us creating conditions in which manufacturers are able to succeed. For them, what that means is a good tax environment, an environment in which they have export opportunities offshore, and our work with the UAE on trade missions with India is really relevant for their future prospects. It's also about ensuring that they can rely on good infrastructure, that we are continuing to invest in the renewable energy

assets that will lead to more affordable electricity in the long run. It is about things such as our work to progress rates caps, which ultimately, rates are a cost that face manufacturers just as they face households. So I'm confident that we are creating the business conditions which will allow manufacturers to succeed into the future. Awesome.

Yeah. So, in the budget, you've set aside $815,000 just transitions for those people that you've said you're sad for because of the loss of their jobs. Is that the sum total of the investment into that transition work? Well, I think the member has a very narrow view of how an economy works, because ultimately what we want to see is that we have an economy where there are opportunities for existing manufacturers to expand, for new manufacturers to establish, so that more jobs are created in that sector, in that industry for all New Zealanders.

What we also want to see is an economy that's dynamic, which is to say that there are more jobs and opportunities being created in new places. And so what I'm buoyed by is the fact that overall, with the economic foundations that our budget puts in place, we are forecasting 220,000 jobs being created, new businesses being created, exports expanding, and businesses continuing to grow.

Because what that tells me is that in the coming months and years, there will be more opportunities in communities. And that is not to say that there haven't been significant setbacks, that there have been business failures. That is the case. But as we look to the future, we can see stronger foundations from which opportunities will grow. Scott, you have a primary finish? Yeah. Scott Willis.

Yes. Thank you. And when you were talking about investment in renewable electricity generation- Mm... or in lowering electricity prices in particular, government's put forward a 200 million gas security fund, but is also funding gas transition, transition away from gas. Do you think there's some inconsistency, given that we know that we don't have any more gas- Mm... to put money into something that is a dead end, while also funding that dead end away from that?

Well, respectfully- Is this a sensible use of public money? Yes, we-- yes, it is. And respectfully, I don't think it's fair to say that gas is a dead end in New Zealand. As much as Jacinda Ardern and James Shaw and Holly Walker tried to make it a dead end, the reality is that the choice to ban oil and gas exploration provided a massive Confidence not to those seeking to explore for natural gas, but it is the case that many experts will tell you that there are likely more significant gas reserves in New Zealand.

The challenge has been twofold. One, the ban has put off people exploring for those gas reserves and investing and extracting them because they're worried that a future leftist government would again make a crazy decision to ban that extraction. Two, natural gas, which has, in the past, provided a nationally resilient sovereign source of energy for New Zealand, has depleted at a faster rate than was previously expected. And that has had a very significant impact, not only on natural gas supplies for manufacturers using it directly,

but also on wholesale energy prices. So our response to those two problems is twofold. On the one hand, we are trying to create more confidence and sovereign confidence in our people investing in natural gas exploration and extraction in New Zealand, and that's what the $200 million fund is about. At the same time, we're being realistic about the fact that the rapidly diminishing supplies of gas in the very short and medium term is such that providing some support to firms previously reliant on gas to transition to electricity is a positive step

to take, not only for those firms who will get lower cost finance for making that transition, but also every time a firm transitions away from natural gas to electricity, that means there's more gas available in the market for those who have no choice but to use gas for their manufacturing processes. So is the minister pleased to be able to adopt the Giddy fund redux to ensure that industry can- I would never go near Giddy with a barge pole, and please never compare anything I have done with it... support the transition away from gas.

No. I would never take the Giddy approach of massive multimillion-dollar free checks of corporate welfare to big businesses to pad their pockets. Instead, what we've taken is a very prudent approach, which says that we can use a Crown guarantee to create lower cost finance for firms that wish to invest their own money in productive assets that will lower their costs over the medium to long term, and that's the approach that the gas transition loan scheme takes.

Dr. David Wilson, your primary. Yeah. Well, thank you, Chair. Well, it'd be for me to talk about corporate welfare. Just wondering whether or not the review has been done into the subsidy that's provided to the film industry, as promised to the committee earlier. And if so, was there anything to be taken from it?

Yeah. Look, as members will recall, we had a look at the overall film subsidy, and consulted with the sector, who gave us feedback that using the same envelope of money, we could get more impact if we made some small changes to it, and we announced those changes last year.

They related to allowing more people access to the 5% uplift and the rebate, particularly for those doing post-development work, and changes in the thresholds for who could apply to it. Now, I've got a particular page in here that I'm trying to turn to, but someone might- Which one? Page 21.

There has been some good evidence that those changes have had a positive impact. Here we go. As at April 2026, 14 productions had registered under the new criteria, including four international and 10 PDV projects. Those productions are expected to generate about 580 million in qualifying New Zealand production expenditure and about 145 million in rebate payments. And looking back over the past decade, the number of screen firms has grown by 38%, mainly in post-production and visual effects, which is emerging as a real specialty for New Zealand, which

again supports high-paid jobs, and really good careers for New Zealanders. So supplementary. Yeah. I'm guessing from your answer that the main evaluation has been around economic impact- Mm... rather than cost benefit. Yes, there has also been cost benefit analysis, which shows that, essentially, the cost benefit analysis, I don't have the specific numbers here, is that for the dollars that we invest through the rebate, we get a multiple of return in the form of investment into the New Zealand economy, creation of jobs, and impact that

would not otherwise have occurred. The film sector is particular in that internationally it is just a heavily, heavily subsidized sector. So basically, if you want to show up to the game, you need to have something there. Now, my view is that New Zealand should never be racing to the bottom, competing, saying, "We'll give you the biggest subsidy."

Actually, our offering should be, "This is the best place in the world to make films because we have the most stunning natural environment, the most talented people," whether it's Peter Jackson or Taika Waititi, "and we also have good facilities. We're a good place to do business. We're not going to tie you up in crazy red tape. And yeah, by the way, we also have a rebate.

It's not as generous as some others, but it's sufficient that you should give us a look." Dr. Vanessa Baining. Thank you very much. Minister, there's about $52 million that are allocated for capital expenditure in the previous budget, and I think it's the same for this budget. What are your priorities for that capital expenditure at MB?

Yeah. So do you mean the overall capital expenditure for the Crown or you were just meaning for MB? Well, just for MB. Yeah, just for MB. Yeah. I'm going to actually ask you, Nick, to speak to the capital investments to MB over the coming year. Obviously, it's always a combination of- Is that part of the- Sorry? I was going to ask, is that part of the write-off for- No. Well, the new investments will be a combination of asset upgrades, technology upgrades, and other upgrades.

But Nick, if I can get you to speak to that. Yeah. No. I think Paul may have the full list, but it'll be A combination of buildings, equipment fit out for offices, it'll be fleet. What's interesting in the technology space is that you're increasingly seeing a shift from large capital investments to more operating. Mm.

So over time, you'll see less of that capital allocation going towards technology projects. Paul, do you have a more- I think that's correct. And just for clarity, the biometric project was not a capital. Okay. That was an operating model, so it's not included in that capital project. As Nick says, a lot of the work that we do, for capital is actually IT. We lease most of our properties, with the exception of the Mangere Refugee Centre, which we do own, and we're doing property upgrades in that space, as you'd imagine.

We are forecasting over the next four or five years, we'll have a steady reduction in capital expenditure as we move our IT to software as a service and technology as a service onto the cloud. So you see a much smaller capital program, but we need to balance that out with increased opex. Mm. Okay. All right. New question. David Benson.

Thank you, Madam Chair. Subsidies. You've talked about the screen production rebate, the gaming development sector rebate. Mm. You've seen enormous success, you would agree, from that rebate, as far as the growth of that industry? Oh, look, I'm really pleased with the growth that's occurred in that industry, 40% year on year. So that's great to see.

Fantastic. Did you consider lowering the minimum annual spend for thresholds for companies to be eligible for the rebate? No. Why was that? As you've just acknowledged, it's already working. Taxpayer dollars are very precious. Every dollar that we spend has an opportunity cost. Every dollar spent is a dollar that would otherwise be spent in our schools and our hospitals on public infrastructure. And so in a very tight fiscal environment, where we have a scheme that's already working and already supporting growth, it hasn't

been a priority to expand it. So part of the strength of that scheme is that it stops companies leaving New Zealand. Have you conducted any work or seen any research to suggest that the settings as they are currently are sufficient to stop some of our most successful companies leaving? Well, as the member literally acknowledged in the framing for your question, we've had 40% growth in the sector year on year, which would suggest that it's a sector that's doing very well.

Yes. And are you aware of the rebates and enticements being offered by other countries for very successful companies in New Zealand to move offshore and potentially have better support from other governments? Similar to the film subsidy, I'm aware that there will always be countries that, for many reasons, can afford to be more generous.

Sometimes because they've had more fiscally disciplined governments in the past that have put them in a stronger position than New Zealand finds itself in after six years of a labor government. Sometimes because they are making different trade-offs than we're making. As we entered into this budget, and in fact each of the three budgets, we have had on our hands a massive task of repairing the deficit left to us by the last government as a structural impediment.

Overcoming the debt mountain, which had exploded and more than doubled while Labor led the government. And we have been having to overcome those two things while also making essentially essential investments in critical public services in our schools and our hospitals and worn down public infrastructure. And we have been prioritizing those things, and at the same time have sustained what is a generous rebate for the gaming sector. And I am really pleased to see the success of that sector. I am very pleased to see it working, and I haven't

seen any evidence to suggest that there is a pressing need to reform those settings when we are achieving so much growth within that sector. Sure. So in that answer, you're talking about responsible fiscal management of government expenditure- Mm... keeping a tight lid on expenditure. Are you happy that a minister in your government overspent by more than $30,000 on a four-day trip overseas?

Madam Chair, I'm not sure that that's within the- Yeah. All right ... jurisdiction of this committee. Do you have any more questions? I've got more primary questions. Okay. We move on to Pushta Ganghi Manu. Thank you, Madam Chair. Minister, do you remain committed to doubling the Maori economy by 2035? And if so, do you have any targeted policies or will you introduce any targeted policies to achieve that?

Well, one of the things that's really compelling about the Maori economy is the significant growth that has been occurring within it. And there's two parts to that. Partly that growth has been tied to the fact that we have seen so much success happening in our primary industries, and you do see that a number of Maori businesses are either whenua related or are related to exporting food, fiber, other products of the land. Equally, we've seen diversification of the Maori economy.

We're increasing rates of Maori entrepreneurship in terms of small businesses owned by Maori, in which there are Maori workers have been flourishing. And so I think it's wrong to kind of say, well, the Maori economy is always a primary industry based economy. That's not what the data supports. Equally, when we look at our goal of export double, over the next decade, which we're by the way, on track with because we've had significant export growth, in recent years.

I see Maori exporters as being a huge part of that, both in terms of ownership of farms, but also value added products. And many of those exporters are supported directly by NZTE, who work with focus businesses that they think have the scope to go global to provide them connections into international markets. An example that comes to mind for me, who I've visited in Whakatane is Waimanuka.

Some members will have tried the product. It's a fizzy honey drink. It's really delicious, and by the way, it's good with whiskey. And look, they've been working with NZTE to get that product to market. And a big part of the provenance story, which they're selling when you buy that product in New York or elsewhere, is that it's got this unique Maori cultural identity behind it.

And I think that for a modern day consumer who's prepared to spend a lot of money on a premium product, that cultural identity story has some value. So I've got confidence that the Maori economy will grow, but I think a large part of that will be through export, not just domestic businesses. I actually used to work with Joe- Ah... who developed Waimaanuka, so- Wonderful man.

Yeah. I can testify. But that said, all the acknowledgments you've made, like I said with progressive procurement, that gave a guaranteed chunk of the pie to ensure that development. So will you introduce any... You've mentioned exports. Mm-hmm. A portion, some export targets for Māori business.

Well, as I say, I'm seeing so much success across the Māori economy that I am confident that those businesses, without a quota, will still be getting support from government agencies. I know that NZTE is already working with many Māori businesses. I know that the changes our government is making across the board are very facilitative of Māori business.

One of the things that I think is interesting with the FastTrack Act is that actually some of the applicants for having projects fast-tracked are iwi or iwi-led organizations, and that's because we have iwi with significant amounts of capital who want to get on and build things and develop things on their land with their resources.

And often it's not so much that they're coming to the government saying, "Here's my cat, can you put something in it?" They're actually saying, "Government, can you get out of the way? You're putting red tape in the way. You're stopping us from doing the development, the innovation, the entrepreneurship that we would like to be doing." And so our government's approach is to say, wherever we can, let's remove those barriers, and I'm very confident that that approach supports all businesses, Māori and non-Māori.

Member Davidson. Thank you. Wanted to talk about artificial intelligence. What was the department's AI spend for the last two years, and what's budgeted going forward for AI in '26? Again, I have a good couple of pages on this. So obviously, many staff at MBIE have access to Copilot, and I get feedback that they are seeing as an increasingly important tool for their work.

AI is also deployed in some of the systems through which they deliver services to New Zealanders, so it is a feature of some of the immigration system in terms of the checks that need to be carried out there. And also it is something where MBIE, I believe, have done pilots with some of their call centers to provide better logs of calls, better quality assurance.

I am... Does someone know which page it is in my folder? I'm flicking through. 36. 36. There you go. No minister can operate without a great team. And so in terms of AI, the overall figure, as I said, the ministry has deployed several AI tools, Microsoft Copilot, Copilot Chat, Copilot Researcher, Genesys Cloud Supervisor AI, internal training being delivered to introduce staff to key AI concepts, and as MBIE is developing a regular reporting on the AI program of work, including investment plans for the future. And when they do those plans, that

includes the AI, the adoption, the risks and the issues. Since early '25, almost half of MBIE staff have had access to Copilot to chat, and usage was showing strong and growing engagement with that. They also have a capability program, which they are building skills through MBIE. And in terms of delivery, a key customer-facing example is the integration of AI into the call center platform, as I talked about, Genesys Cloud, and that's about automated call summarization, real-time note generation, voice-enabled IVR and chatbots. And the benefits for the New Zealander on the other end of

that include reduced wait times, clearer insights into what they're actually trying to achieve, and more streamlined processes. AI is also being embedded into the Nexus ministerial services workflow tool, and policy teams are using AI to accelerate some of their work. Right. Can you tell me more about what that voice-enabled IVR is?

The call center you're talking about? Yeah. Yeah. I can say a bit about that. It's similar to what other departments like IR and ACC have been doing, so it's being able to listen to the call effectively, do the transcript, and do the summary notes. So those are the bits that can take quite a bit of time at the end of a call for case managers. And so being able to do that quickly has helped save time, makes it more efficient. So that's the pilots.

We're more in the pilot phase of that at the moment. Sure. And just further to that, because the question was around the cost- Mm-hmm... both incurred thus far and forecast into the future for those, and there was a bit of detail in the answer, but there wasn't a dollar figure attached. So I don't have the full data that you're asking for. I do know that the ministry's purchase of 970 Microsoft Copilot 365 licenses came in at a cost of $423,289.50 in the '25, '26 year. That's excluding GST.

In terms of overall AI spend, I'm very happy to come back to the member in writing. And do you suspect, or do you know whether there's an increase in that spend or forecast expenditure? I don't know, but I would expect there to be. Because the way that I conceptualize AI is if we think about the past 20 years, what we have seen across New Zealand businesses and public service agencies is increasing proportion of investment happening in digital tools. Why?

To ensure better service to the public, and to assure more efficient use of taxpayer resources. As we look to the next decade AI is, in a sense, the next wave of digital tools. It's about how do we get better services for people, better informed services, more responsive services, more tailored services, but do that in a way that achieves maximum value for money.

And so where AI can be used to support a better outcome for the New Zealander interacting with government, then it is something that we expect government agencies to consider, but to do so very mindfully. All of it needs to be used within an ethical framework with data protections, privacy protections, and in a way that is always tested against, okay, well, if the hoped-for benefit was that it would make it better for the consumer on the other end, did it achieve that?

Sure. And that's good to hear, because that mindful approach is something we're hearing more and more is required- Absolutely... especially from the countries that are ahead of us. Yes. Are you satisfied that you've got appropriate safeguards in place? I am satisfied that government agencies take those protections and controls very seriously, both in terms of the governance of AI, the data protocols, and the privacy protocols.

I also fully expect that we need to keep retesting those. There are principles and policies in place. Making the Public Service Commissioner now the overall head of digital for the public service, I think is important, because as we look to the future, making sure that we have ethical rules which keep up to date with technology development, I think does require a center of accountability. And I think it's appropriate that the leader of the public service is the leader of that accountability, because this is a technology that is changing extremely fast.

And so we can't stay still. We can't say, "Look, we've done it. We've ticked that box. We've got all the protections and protocols in place," because I fully expect they will continue to need to evolve as the technology evolves. So just to final up on that, I think. The people who do those jobs, who work in that space with AI, with tech, with- Mm... all the areas that you've talked about being growth areas, have traditionally been known as back room or back office roles.

Mm. You've been very vocal and confident about the need to reduce back office roles. Mm-hmm. How does that marry up with what you're talking about today, about the need for significant and serious investment in exactly the same space? Because what we're working to achieve is that we get more impact for every dollar that we're investing.

And so if what I can have is one person in a call center who is doing a beautiful job of empathetically engaging with the people who call them, but instead of then having to spend half their time taking call logs, analyzing previous notes, writing synthesis and analysis, and instead an AI tool does that for them, then effectively I'm doubling their productivity.

So what that means is that we can make AI investments that mean that people in administrative and support roles can be more effective with the resources that they have. Which should mean, as we look to the future, it's very realistic to say that the New Zealand Public Service can be proportionately more like it's been in the past, which is to say around 1% of the population. It exploded to 1.2% under the last government, and we don't think it's unrealistic to say that over the next few years we can get it back to 1%, given all of the modern tools

available to us. Thank you for the example. It's a good one. We all dream of days where we have wonderful, friendly, and available people in call centers to- ... press when you dial the number. Yeah. But I would argue that a call center operator is a frontline role, and in fact, the systems and services that they rely on, be it AI or IT, require a level of human engagement and interaction to ensure that they are operating and that they are safe.

So in your example, the risk is that it doesn't account for the human resource still required to ensure those systems are operating with the kind of integrity that I think all responsible governments would want to know is the case. Well, you can take a glass half empty approach, as is sort of your role in opposition, I guess. But my approach is to say, actually, how do we ensure a better service to the public with better value for money? And I think that the use of AI gives us way more options about how we do that.

And often actually, it is welcomed by workers themselves. The example I'd give you of that was the trial that Health New Zealand ran of using AI scribes in emergency rooms where, for many years, nurses and health practitioners, doctors have said, "Look, the time you have to spend in the emergency room taking notes, uploading them, getting them into the right systems, is time that directly takes you away from being with the people who are waiting in the queue in the emergency room." And of course, that's what doctors and nurses want to be doing.

They want to be helping people, not sitting at a computer typing stuff in. So the AI scribe that was trialed in those emergency rooms freed up literally hours each week that nurses and doctors were then able to spend face-to-face with patients rather than in admin. And so I think it's no surprise that the survey feedback, when those practitioners were then asked, "Well, how did you feel about this AI tool?" They were overwhelmingly positive because it actually let them focus on the part of their job that they come to work to do, which is to work with patients.

And so I think there's a win-win opportunity with AI, which is to say, how can we make work more rewarding and more productive by getting these modern dot-digital tools to help with some of the parts of the task that actually a computer can do, and focus our human capability and talent on the bits that only humans can do. Right. A supplement to Dr. Davidson.

Yeah, just a brief one. Very good to hear all the gains and advances in AI. Along with LLMs and Scribe and the like, there's a co-requisite- issue that we need to take care of, and that's data sovereignty. Mm-hmm. Can you tell us or assure us that we're building those things as we go? I'm conscious that for the overall public service, those questions of our AI protocol systems practices sit with the Minister for, Public Services and Digital, rather than me.

So I don't want to speak for his portfolio. But what I can have confidence in is that, MBIE is fully subscribing to all of those protocols in its work, and that it tests them against them. I'd invite you, Nick, if you've got any comments to make about the frameworks and what you're, trialing in using AI.

Yeah. Just to the minister's point, we absolutely work closely with what's now the GDDA to make sure that we're abiding by all those. But just to give another couple examples, submissions analysis is another area that we've been, trialing again to speed up that process. There's still a really strong role for how do you analyze and set up the framing, but then allowing a computer to help with that.

Ministerial correspondence, again, that's an area where we've been piloting work as well. We have recently released the last report on the IVL investment plan, in terms of the backward-looking one. And there's also information on the MB website about IVL investments. But, clearly we need to do a better job of making it clear where that is to stakeholders, because I've had the concern raised by stakeholders and- Mm... and that surprised me.

So Nick and the team are well aware of the fact that we need to do a better job of providing greater transparency about where the IVL funding is going, when it was committed, when the project is being delivered, and those sorts of things. And also, I want to see greater measurement of impact.

So cycle trails, we've got really clear economic impact from investments in cycle trails. As I said before, the event review will be able to look at where have our investments been in events, what has that delivered in terms of overall impact.

Then on individual events within the event boost, we'll be looking at economic impact, as we do with the major events. The event attraction package will do that as a collective. And that's on the basis that we do want to ensure that there is value for the public, value for taxpayers in the investments that we're making.

But yep, fair cop, I take the feedback that it hasn't been easy enough for people to see where that IVL investment has gone. And can I just-- I agree. There's a lot there. It's one of those situations where there's almost too much. It's not easy to navigate. So absolutely, we've heard that feedback, and we're working on how we can make that better and be more proactive with our communication out to RTOs and other stakeholders.

Mm. Again, I guess it's the sentiment I'm talking about in terms of people out there seeing that as a government, as a cabinet, you cut money to tourism, and that's what people see, and then they feel like-- I guess the pure version of the IVL was that people believed it was going into building pathways and bathrooms, and now it's being used to fund Tourism New Zealand, which desperately needs the money.

So I guess that's the challenge I'm putting to you. Can you not see it seems like you're saying, "Hey, let's go for growth," but then we cut money, we put some money somewhere else, and it doesn't seem like you are as committed as- Yeah. So-... you say you are... so let me-- and estimates is normally forward-looking. But let me just traverse that little piece first.

So when we came into office, yes, there was an expectation that every government agency, and organizations looked at savings because we needed to. In terms of overall government spending, it was unsustainable, and in order to get inflation back under control, we had to look at savings everywhere. That happened.

In that, in terms of the international visitor levy, it was very clear when we increased it that every dollar would be for tourism and conservation. Tourism and hospitality, they'd love that. Tourism and conservation. But actually, there was no kind of comment publicly that it was all going into new supply initiatives.

And so that was-- people may have thought that, but there was no basis for that consideration. What I have done since I've come into the role is been very clear about the percentage and the allocation of the 35 million of new spending, and getting the balance right. So yes, new spending will be required for growth. But the majority of RTOs still tell me their biggest problem is they have capacity, their businesses have capacity, they want more customers, they want more visitors, and that is what we have been focusing on as a first priority,

while at the same time building the system underneath or considering the system underneath, so that as those numbers grow, we're in a strong position to manage those increased numbers. As I've said, and I'm sure I said it here last year, I'm very acutely aware of the discomfort that can come from growth that isn't managed. And in 2019, in some quarters there was some concern, and we can't do that. So currently, 95% visitor community satisfaction with visitors,

and we need to maintain that level of social license. I have also said in my new performance expectations from Tourism New Zealand- That they are considering regional dispersal, and they are considering social license. So those are two new changes in what I have set as expectations to the Board of Tourism New Zealand for that very reason.

So, I want you to be reassured that I have been listening and I have heard. It is not growth at any cost. It is growth that is managed, that we invest in to grow, and then as the numbers grow, invest in managing that growth. And so on that, and I get you.

The looking back bit was really around the, I guess the setting the scene of how you fight in this budget, for the dispersal of the IVL funding. So I'm assuming you took it to Cabinet and made recommendations of this is where the IVL should go, to here, here, and here, or we should keep it here.

No. So, two things. Firstly, I'll add with the major event and tourism package, there was additional funding put in that was outside the IVL. And can I tell you, squeezing any new money out of the system is a very rare feat. Mm. But that was done very deliberately and carefully.

Then, IVL ministers, the Minister of Finance, myself, and the Minister of Conservation. So, we put together proposals. My proposals fit within the percentages that I've been clear about with the sector. Take those and look at alignment with the Minister of Conservation first and foremost, because where possible, we want to make sure that we are leveraging that investment like we did last year with significant investments in Milford.

And then a conversation with the Minister of Finance, and that's where the final approval process is. Just a couple of stops on the IVL. Thank you. With the projected income increase from the IVL forecasted to grow from 190 to about 229, do you expect or have any appetite to revise the proportion of that that goes to baselines as opposed to other initiatives?

Well, the decision was made before my time. But part of what the Minister of Finance was wanting to do was to provide certainty around the 90 million that was available for new funding. But I can promise you, I have started the conversations and will continue, that as the visitor numbers grow, I will be advocating very strongly, and have been already, that the increase in revenue, the new spending, should reflect- Right part of that increase.

And given that the Electrifying Great Rides had a due date of 2024/2025, and it's not yet complete, will this year's budget allocation from the IVL finish the job? Yes. Well, that was a contestable process. I think there were some areas that were slower to express interest.

And we have now, I think somebody will need to correct me in terms of what month we will finish that work. David? Next year they'll be finished. So they'll be contacted with second round, and they've all been contacted now, and an expectation from contract is within 18 months. Just another one.

Another one, yeah. If I may. Minister, do you expect that the continued funding of the Ngā Haerenga cycle rides through the IVL will expand the number of rides and work towards connecting the main centers? Or what expectations do you have around this? Yes. So I partially answered some of that with Dr. Weinig's question. There's this live debate. So one of the things we did last year was allocated some of the funding for management and maintenance of the rides, because what we were hearing from the organizations that ran the Great Rides was

they wanted to be able to ensure they could fund management and maintenance. So we did ring-fence some of the funding within the 8 million to do that. And then the big question is, do you continue extending the existing 23 Great Rides and fill the gaps, or do you look at new rides?

I have been unpopular with some because I've said, "Actually, we should focus on the 23," and doing the extensions or filling the gaps. The Central Plateau one is a great example. Some of those areas are long and expensive, but we do want to cover that.

The Dunedin Tunnel Trails are doing great work, so we're also trying to extend that so that over time we absolutely do deliver that vision of Great Rides from one end of New Zealand, not just down, but round the country. But it's great that more and more communities want to build them.

Mm-hmm. But then there is also the flip side of co-funding. And it's not just councils, it's iwi, it's community donations, and others that are helping to fund those Great Rides. But they've been wildly successful. Mm. I think at the very first one that we launched in 2009, with the then Green Party MP, because that was part of our agreement with the Green Party in that term of Parliament Fantastic Thank you. And, yes, you are the Minister for Tourism and Hospitality, and I would probably be on pot washing duties if I didn't ask some questions.

Sure. I'm sure they're watching intently. No. But often they are seen as the sort of forgotten- Yeah... brother or cousin. You mentioned at the start of your opening around the Minister of Regulations doing the hospitality review. Mm-hmm. Can you indicate anything else specifically that Budget 2026 is doing to support hospitality in New Zealand?

Well, obviously the bringing the Michelin Guide to New Zealand is the biggest one, and we will launch the guide at the end of this month. And when we announced that was coming to New Zealand, because that directly came out of the 2024 Hospitality Summit that was hosted by my predecessor here in Parliament, and that was on the list of asks.

And I remember when we launched it in Auckland last year, because that was, what, 11 months after? Ten months after? Something like that, yeah. And talking to one of the key people in the sector and saying, "How does it feel now that we've announced it?" And he said to me it was a pipe dream.

He didn't think we would ever have Michelin in New Zealand. And that said a lot to me because, it is really important for those across our hospitality sector to know that they can match any top restaurant around the world. And it's not just for the top restaurants.

One of the things that I don't think has been understood yet in terms of the size of the opportunity is when we profile New Zealand food in a Michelin star restaurant, it's also speaking to the quality of the food that is produced by farmers and growers all over New Zealand.

And I see a really direct opportunity then for the Fonterras, for the Red Meat Group, for the Zespris, for our winemakers, to then use that to build trade exports overseas. And so the trade missions I was on with the Prime Minister last year, that is absolutely it.

So, it is having the Olympics, if you like, for hospitality. So if you are a young person deciding, and actually met some of these people when we launched it, some of the pastry chefs, young men and women who were just doing absolutely phenomenal work, them then knowing that they could achieve a standard in New Zealand that was world-leading, and that they could then work in a restaurant, world-leading anywhere in the world based on their training and the skills and work experience they developed here at home.

And I think that's really important for us to raise the aspiration and the opportunities for New Zealand workers. I do feel there's a disconnect, though. And I think of a mum and dad cafe owner in Wanganui or Wellington or Wanaka who are struggling every day to just get enough customers through the door.

And you would have known the numbers around, so record liquidations of hospitality businesses to March this year. And I guess again, in terms of, yep, that's really wonderful, and I know that there is slowdown, as you said, but in terms of just those small hospitality operators, what is it you can offer them or guarantee them this budget-... is actually going to support them going forward?

So here's the interesting thing. So, in the year to May, hospitality spend was up 5.9% on the year before. So, May 26 compared to May 25 is up 5.9%. The number of accommodation and food service businesses grew by 2.6%. So although we're reading about liquidations, and they're heartbreaking.

Mm. Anybody who's started a business who was then in a position where they have to liquidate, it's hideous. It's hideous for them, for their staff, for their customers, and their suppliers, right? But we are seeing an increase of about 55 businesses month on month. So there is still confidence in the sector to be able to start new businesses.

When we deliver the findings from the regulations review, as I said, led by Minister Seymour, there will be practical things in that, that we remove red tape, that enables them to operate their businesses more easily. They're not components of the budget.

The hospitality summit that we did this year, I've not yet received the report back from Hospitality New Zealand in terms of what they see the priorities are, but we'll continue to work with them on delivering the priority ones. And actually, a lot of them weren't about money.

Mm. They were about rules, they were about regulations. Actually, many of them were about relationships, and it was really good, they said, to have representatives of different government agencies sitting at the table with them at the summit, being able to understand both sides of the equation.

So whether it was MPI and Food Safety, and the businesses being able to understand why MPI do things a certain way, they were able to then say, "Well, this is the impact on us." And so, that relationship and that connection is also really valuable But we'll see what's going to happen from Hospitality New Zealand's feedback from the summit, and Restaurants Association feedback from the summit, put that into an action plan.

And then also once we get the final list of actions out of the regulations review, that'll be the work program that will shift the dial for hospitality. Good. All good? Mm-hmm. Oh, you have- Got one new primary if anyone wants to- New primary, yep. We do have time.

Boy, then. I do want to come to the Major Event Development Fund. Mm-hmm. And again, I think murky is another word that some people-- I don't know if the word secretive is the right word, but again, around people being invited, only invited to apply, that type of thing.

Again, reassurance from-- Again, as I get around, people just don't quite understand how they can actually get themselves in, and then how the decisions are made. So can you give us some reassurance that, I guess, around bang for buck, around making sure that the right people, that it's not all North Island-centric.

I know there is something in the South Island. But the fact that it is actually, obviously, the accountability that you have as the minister, that money is being well spent. Yep, absolutely. So, the major events and tourism package has several components. We'll focus on the event side. Forty million was the event attraction package.

Ten million for event boost. Event boost was more straightforward. That was really kind of focused more on regional level events. Didn't have to be a new event. Had to be delivered within a certain period of time. If it was an existing event, part of the criteria was it had to have a new element that could or would attract international visitation.

So, in terms of the numbers, we had-- I don't have the detail. There were about 200, somebody correct me if I'm wrong, about 200 applications to the event boost fund. Thirty-six events confirmed. And that was a group that considered those applications based on the criteria people applying to the fund.

A lot of them, over half of them, weren't eligible, didn't fulfill the criteria, so they were off the table straight away. And then the recommendations were put forward as to which ones. We looked at the regional spread and said, "Yes, go." So 21 events of those have been delivered or are in delivery. And we will have economic outcomes from those. The event attraction panel was-- The event attraction package was very deliberately different.

We wanted to try something different from the existing major events. And from some quarters, there was significant concern about events that weren't eligible within the current major events criteria. And so this was the opportunity to widen the scope.

And so, I had a panel of experts, individuals who work in the event sector, who had particular expertise, who we deliberately asked to go out and find new events. And the World Surfing League is a perfect example. They would never have come to us-... applied for the major events.

Snow League in Wanaka is probably another example that wouldn't have come through the major events process. But we went out, or the panel went out hunting for new events. They then provided recommendations to me on return on investment, geographic spread, seasonal spread, the genre of events. So they weren't all concerts, they weren't all sports events. Some of them were cultural events and exhibitions, broad section.

I have not made one decision that was outside what that panel recommended to me. Okay. Right? So this view that I've sat around and dreamt up events is entirely wrong. I have just followed the advice of this group of experts. And then what we want to do at the end of it, because there are components that are commercially sensitive, and bluntly, we are competing with Australia. Mm.

Australia's states don't disclose the values of those investments. What we will do at the end of it is put them all together and say, "This is the economic impact delivered from this investment." And it will then determine what we do with the event strategy going forward. And we might say concerts delivered whatever amount per dollar investment, and that's totally worth continued investment.

It might say that this other type of event- Right ... was a bomb, and we shouldn't be doing that in future. And, yep, that's on me, and I'm happy to be in a position where we have invested in a much wider range of events across the country than we would have otherwise.

And as I said, I think the numbers when they come in, World Surf League, within a day of the event starting- The organizers were like, "Wow, what an amazing place for us to hold this event." And part of the bit that's harder to measure, and I'm looking to Renee for this, is you might have, say, 15,000, 20,000 physical visitors at an event, but millions and millions who watched the event either through TV or social media.

World Surfing League is in the top five sports of international social media reach. Let's see your help too. And so the value of that for New Zealand, we'll attempt to measure it. Mm. But it is actually quite hard to do. Do you want to- Yeah... do you want to add to that?

Yeah. Certainly when you look at events, those are the two key metrics, the number of people who will come for the event, and then the media eyeballs that can give you the reach for future visitation. And what we do with the major events is really look at the media interest and the people who come here and host them beyond the event.

They'll cover the event anyway, that's why they're here. But we will help them fill the time in between the event so they get a broader view of New Zealand, what it's like to visit New Zealand. And we can measure that in a number of ways. Estimated advertising value, if I was to buy that space, it would cost me X amount of dollars. So there's various ways that you can measure that.

But certainly, those are the two key metrics is visitors on the ground for the event, and then viewership, and estimated advertising value from people watching the event. Mm. Finals- Yeah. I guess, and I hear what you're saying, and I get with the commercially sensitive thing, and so you're going to package it all up, and there might be a dud or two.

But will it mean you'll get away with sort of tucking it into just a general report? Or like for example, the music industry in New Zealand, frustrated, right? Because we're paying for international artists to come here, and we're not backing them as we could be. And so will we know- We're not paying the artist. So let me be clear, we're not paying the artist.

What the recognition was, was for some artists, Linkin Park sold out that arena, had the highest audience they've ever had in, it's not Spark Arena anymore. What's it called? Anyway, doesn't matter. Doesn't matter. And so they wouldn't have come to New Zealand without us providing some financial support, which covers the costs of them traveling to get here, right?

So the money does not go into the artists' pockets. One of the things we're looking at, which again, has been feedback we've received, is can we provide greater opportunity through that international artists to then have the pre-performer or the warm-up performer as local.

What is also not well represented is every component of that concert supports New Zealand businesses. The lighting, the riggers, the staging, the caterers, buses, taxis, the hotels that look after the artists, you name it. That all goes into the pockets of New Zealand businesses. And one of the early comments I heard from the events sector more broadly is, it's really hard for those businesses to survive if they're only doing three or four big events a year.

But if they've got some international conferences that they're doing, and they're doing a stage show, and they're doing these concerts, and they're doing the Chiefs hurricanes, go the Chiefs, just quietly. If they're doing a wide range, then it means that their businesses are more stable and successful, and they have the confidence to invest, take on staff, et cetera.

So it's those sorts of benefits that, probably to be fair, that we didn't communicate well enough early on. All right. Minister, we'll have to conclude there. Thank you so much for your time. Thank you. And we will get some written questions later on. Sure. Thank you.
Thank you for your time. Hope the rest of your afternoon goes well.
